coredata.dc:title: Photo-stimuli responsive phosphorescence from carbon dots in porous gelatin
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ruochen
Wang, Yi
Li, Qijun
Sun, Guoming
coredata.dc:description: Stimuli-responsive phosphorescent materials with dynamic phosphorescent property have great potential in multi-level anti-counterfeiting and high-capacity storage applications. However, achieving such materials still is challenging in that there is no rational design or manufacture method yet. In this work, we report for the first time a facile method to tune the pore size of gelatin film by controlling annealing temperature, and then achieve photo-induced phosphorescence of Carbon dots (CDs) in porous gelatin film. The fabricated CDs@Gelatin film can display spatially accurate photoinduced dynamic phosphorescence and their phosphorescence can be activated after photo-irradiation for a few seconds and de-activated by placing in air for a period of time. Our data indicate that dynamic phosphorescence is mainly related to the special micro-nano porous structure of gelatins which is formed during the water evaporation progress. Under light irradiation, triplet excitons generated by CDs embedded in porous gelatin matrix can consume triplet oxygen, leading to phosphorescence photoactivation. When oxygen re-enter the porous CDs@Gelatin film, the phosphorescence of the material will disappear. Furthermore, the potential applications of this material were demonstrated in optical patterning and data security.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Porous
Gelatin
Photo-stimuli responsive
Phosphorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of CDs@Gelatin film and its photo-stimuli-responsive phosphorescence.

Characterizations of synthesized CDs. (a) TEM image of CDs; (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs; (c) XPS spectra of CDs; (d) HR-XPS C 1s spectrum of CDs and its fitting results; (e) UV–visible absorption spectra of CD aqueous solution, and (f) fluorescence emission spectrum of CD aqueous solution under excitation at 330 nm.

Photophysical properties of CDs@Gelatin film. (a) Phosphorescence emission spectrum of CDs@Gelatin film at room temperature after excitation at 330 nm; (b) phosphorescent decay profile of CDs@Gelatin film; (c) Phosphorescence intensity of three CDs@Gelatin films upon continuous irradiation (excitation at 330 nm, 60 s irradiation followed by 120 s darkness); (d) Phosphorescence emission spectra of CDs@Gelatin film at different temperatures (excitation at 330 nm); (e) Thermogravimetric properties of CDs@Gelatin films at different temperatures; (f) The pore size distribution of two CDs@Gelatin films.

Schematic diagram of photo-stimuli-responsive phosphorescence of CDs@Gelatin film. (a) Schematic illustration of the oxygen-mediated dynamic photo-stimuli-responsive phosphorescence of CDs@Gelatin film; (b) Effect of annealing temperature on the aperture of CDs@Gelatin film.

Illustration of ink-free optical printing of photo-stimuli-responsive phosphorescent patterns using mask and photolithography. (a) Schematic diagram of CDs@Gelatin film used for green screen printing; (b) The pictures of two patterns after removing the excitation source.

head.ce:title: Photo-stimuli responsive phosphorescence from carbon dots in porous gelatin

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Science
Institute of Life Science and Green Development

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Stimuli-responsive phosphorescent materials with dynamic phosphorescent property have great potential in multi-level anti-counterfeiting and high-capacity storage applications. However, achieving such materials still is challenging in that there is no rational design or manufacture method yet. In this work, we report for the first time a facile method to tune the pore size of gelatin film by controlling annealing temperature, and then achieve photo-induced phosphorescence of Carbon dots (CDs) in porous gelatin film. The fabricated CDs@Gelatin film can display spatially accurate photoinduced dynamic phosphorescence and their phosphorescence can be activated after photo-irradiation for a few seconds and de-activated by placing in air for a period of time. Our data indicate that dynamic phosphorescence is mainly related to the special micro-nano porous structure of gelatins which is formed during the water evaporation progress. Under light irradiation, triplet excitons generated by CDs embedded in porous gelatin matrix can consume triplet oxygen, leading to phosphorescence photoactivation. When oxygen re-enter the porous CDs@Gelatin film, the phosphorescence of the material will disappear. Furthermore, the potential applications of this material were demonstrated in optical patterning and data security.

We present a new strategy to control the pore size of gelatin to realize photo-induced phosphorescence of CDs by evaporating water.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Information security aims to protect data and ensure confidentiality of the valuable and crucial information, and it plays extremely important roles in such field as economy [1–3]. In recent years, many information security technologies have been developed, such as luminescent anti-fake labels, barcodes, fingerprint recognition and magnetism [4–7]. Among them, the luminescent material has the advantages of high confidentiality, ease of manipulation, and efficient manufacture [8,9]. Stimuli-responsive materials have drawn special attention to constructing information security because of their unique response behavior [10–16]. Under different external stimuli, their light output can be precisely modulated to produce a rich signal, preventing the message from being read or imitated [17].

Unlike traditional materials, which respond instantly, phosphorescent materials show switchable on-off optical responses with excellent memory effects. Altering the bistable molecular accumulation forms molecular phosphorus under light/thermal stimulation, thereby enabling stimuli-responsive phosphorescent with memory effect [18,19]. Gmelch et al. developed polymer films with optically programmable long afterglow. These successful examples have revolutionized the performance of organic phosphorescent materials, opening up new applications such as multi-level security and dynamic photo-stimuli responsive modeling [20,21].

Carbon dots (CDs) are promising carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials [22], which has been extensively explored as stimulant responsive materials because of their excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, and cost-effective preparation [23–33]. The phosphorescence and delayed fluorescence (FL) properties of CDs can be observed after being embedded in substrates (e.g., boric acid, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), aluminum sulfate (Al2(SO4)3), urea, silica gel) [34–38]. Recent studies have shown that CDs in the matrix can not only act as phosphorescent emitters but also act as the reaction center to change the regional environment in the material, thus producing the dynamic phosphorescent performance. For example, Qu et al. demonstrated that increasing the cross-linking between CDs and PVA via heat treatment successfully triggered phosphorescent enhancement in composite films [39]. Li et al. demonstrated multistimuli (alkali or thermal) and excitation-dependent dynamic phosphorescence colors from CD@paper [2]. Tan et al. achieved dynamic phosphorescence color output based on inner core doping and surface engineering [40]. Most recently, Jia et al. demonstrated an example of photo-induced phosphorescence material made of CDs and porous polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) [15]. Among all reported stimuli-responsive materials, those that have photo-responsive afterglow features are significantly superior than others due to their contactless and remote controllability. However, achieving such materials still is challenging due to the lack of rational design and manufacture method.

Gelatin is a biodegradable protein derived from the partial hydrolysis of collagen, which have been widely used in cosmetics, additives and photography applications [41]. Its unique protein structure determines its excellent biological and physical properties, such as good biocompatibility, sol-gel reversible conversion, biodegradability, and other characteristics. Gelatin with a large protein structure provide a fascinating matrix for stabilizing the triple excited states of CDs to achieve phosphorescence emission. However, related work has not yet been reported.

Here, we report for the first time that gelatin can be used as an effective matrix to achieve photo-induced phosphorescence by embedding CDs into them. We first synthesized CDs via hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea, and then mixed with gelatin in aqueous solution, and heated at specific temperature to obtain a porous and transparent film. Other than photo-stimuli response, the CDs@Gelatin film can display spatially accurate photo-induced dynamic phosphorescence, in which the porous structure of gelatin plays an important role in the photo-stimulating response of the composite film. Our study showed that the annealing temperature can effectively affect the micro pore size of gelatin, and then affect the sensitivity of stimuli response. The photo response of the composite films was optimized at 110 °C so that large number of pore structures is formed in film due to rapid water evaporation, thus allowing efficient oxygen exchange. As a result, the phosphorescence of the CDs@Gelatin film can be activated reversibly by photo-irradiation and de-activated by re-permeated oxygen. Furthermore, potential applications of this material were demonstrated using optical patterning and dynamic encryption.

Anhydrous citric acid (1.0 g) and urea (0.5 g) were first dissolved in ultra-pure water (25 mL). The solution was then transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined High pressure reactor (100 mL) and heated at 230 °C for 8 h. The resulting dark brown solution was centrifuged at high speed (10,000 rad/min) for 20 min to remove large or condensed particles. Subsequently, the supernatant was collected followed by dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500 Da) for 10 h. The final product was obtained by freeze-drying. The resulting stock solution of CDs (1 mg mL−1) was then prepared for further use.

To prepare CDs@Gelatin film, gelatin (1 g) was completely dissolved in deionized water (10 mL) at 368 K (70 °C). After that, the 0.5 mL aqueous solution containing CDs (1 mg mL−1) was added to the gelatin aqueous solution. The solution was further stirred for 30 min to ensure uniform dispersion. Then the evenly mixed solution was poured into the Petri dish and dried at 40 °C, 70 °C, 110 °C, and 170 °C to obtain the brown-yellow film.

The absorption spectra were recorded with shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) absorption spectra were obtained by NICOLET iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific TM, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data, including a combination of energy measurements and high-resolution spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, were obtained on the PHI 5000 Versa probe (ULVAC-Phi, Japan). The phosphorescent spectra were collected on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The phosphorescent spectra were collected on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Rigaku 18 KW D/max-2550 using Cu Ka radiation.

The morphology of the composite film was studied using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, H-7650 instrument, Hitachi, Japan). A diluted aqueous solution of CDs was deposited on a carbon-coated copper grid for TEM.

The phosphorescent lifetime decay curve was measured using an Edinburgh FLS920 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with xenon arc lamp (Xe900) and nanosecond hydrogen flash lamp (nF920). The sample was placed in an Optistat DN-V liquid nitrogen cryostat with a temperature control ranging between 80 and 293 K. The luminescence lifetimes (τ) were calculated by fitting the decay curve with the multi-exponential decay function shown below:
I
( t )
=
∑ i
A i
ⅇ
−
t
τ i

Here, Ai and I stand for the individual component's amplitude and lifetime of a multi-exponential decay curve, respectively. TG was performed on a Rigaku TG-DTA8122 at a cooling and heating rate of 5 K min−1. The pore size analysis report of the sample was obtained from V-SORB 2800P surface area and pore size analyzer of Beijing Jin Ai Pu Technology Co., LTD.

The polymer films with photoresponsive phosphorescence were fabricated as aforementioned (Fig. 1 ). The CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis in the presence of urea and citric acid, followed by mixing them with gelatin at different temperatures. The resulting CDs@Gelatin film initially emitted bright cyan light under 365 nm excitation, but the light disappeared immediately when the irradiation was off. The short period of irradiation (<0.5 s) did not generate any visible phosphorescent emission. Interestingly, a long green afterglow appeared after the film was continuously exposed to a 365 nm UV light for up to 90 s. The fully activated phosphorescence, which was induced by locally photoinduced hypoxia, lasted for a considerable period of time at room temperature and then gradually disappeared due to oxygen infiltration. This on-off switch can repeat multiple cycles without causing significant deterioration in the long afterglow performance.

To determine the properties of CDs within the dynamic stimuli-responsive phosphorescent films, the morphologies of the CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Our data showed that the CDs were uniformly dispersed and quasi-spherical with an average size of about 8.76 nm (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1a). The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. S1b) has a broad peak at around 2θ = 23°, which is corresponded to the typical peak of CDs as previously reported [42].

The surface groups and chemical composition of CDs in stimuli-responsive phosphorescent films were determined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. The CDs have a wide FT-IR absorption band at 2500-3500 cm−1 (containing discernible peaks at 3208, 3045 and 2824 cm−1) (Fig. 2b), which is attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH, –NH2, and –CH2– groups [43,44]. The peak located at 1671 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO, and the absorption peak at 1555 cm−1 and 1393 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of N–H and C–N [45]. These FT-IR results were further confirmed by XPS characterization. The results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) showed that URTP CDs mainly contained C, N, and O elements (Fig. 2c). The HR XPS spectrum of C 1s indicates that C–C/CC (284.6 eV), C–N (285.4 eV), and CO (286.3 eV) bonds exist in CDs (Fig. 2d). N 1s spectrum in XPS spectrum contains three components (Fig. S1c), which are attributed to C–NC (399.7 eV), N-(C)3 (400.6 eV), and N–H (401.3 eV), respectively. The O 1s XPS spectrum has two peaks in deconvolution at 530.6 and 532.4 eV, contributing to the C–OH/C–O–C and O–H bond respectively (Fig. S1d). These findings suggest that O- and N- related groups are abundant on the surface of CDs.

To examine the photopic properties of CDs films, the UV absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in an aqueous solution were tested (Fig. 2e and f). The aqueous dispersions of CDs showed excitation-dependent FL characteristics and had a maximum emission at the optimal excitation wavelength of 330 nm (Fig. 2f). The main absorption peak at about 330 nm was generally attributed to the n → л * transition of some sub-fluorophores contained in CDs [46]. Moreover, CDs@Gelatin film showed the phosphorescence emission with peak at 480 nm (Fig. 3 a), and its average lifetime was 188.83 ms under 365 nm excitation (Fig. 3b). Phosphorescence excitation spectra of CDs@Gelatin film under 480 nm emission was measured. Their excitation bands ranging from 280 nm to 400 nm is overlapped with the absorption band of CDs, suggesting that the phosphorescence emission was mainly originated from the heterocycles of CDs. The gelatin matrix exhibited very weak phosphorescence compared to that of CDs@Gelatin, which indicates that the phosphorescence mainly comes from the contribution of CDs rather than the gelatin (Fig. S2).

To further explore the sensitivity of the photo stimuli-responsive CDs@Gelatin film, we set the film curing temperatures between 40 and 170 °C (Fig. 3c). The phosphorescence of CDs@Gelatin film was reversible in response to photostimulation. When the UV light excitation was stopped for 2 min, the phosphorescence intensity returned to the starting position. The sensitivity of photo stimuli-responsive was optimized at 110 °C. The sensitivity of photo stimulus response is poor when the temperature is below 110 and 170 °C. The main reason for this phenomenon is that when the temperature is lower than 110 °C, the bound water cannot evaporate out, so the gelatin holes are filled with bound water and oxygen cannot enter freely, resulting in poor response. At 170 °C, cross-linking occurs in gelatin, leading to porosity reduction and poor responsiveness. At 110 °C, the bound water is evaporated, leaving large pores, oxygen in and out of the smooth, so the response is good.

The phosphorescence intensity increased first and then decreased with the increase of drying temperature (Fig. 3d). Specifically, the maximum phosphorescence intensity and photo stimuli-responsive sensitivity are reached at the drying temperature of 110 °C. This phenomenon is mainly attributed to the interaction of CDs with the surrounding environment. When drying under the condition of less than 110 °C, the residual water (free water and bound water) in the film can still exist stably, resulting in weak phosphorescence emission (Fig. 3e). However, when drying above 110 °C, the residual water in the film was completely removed, which may lead to the collapse of the gelatin matrix, thus reducing the fixation effect of the matrix on the CDs. Therefore, when drying at 110 °C, a certain amount of bound water still exist the film, which can not only effectively solidify the CO and other functional groups on the CDs surface, but also greatly improve the rigidity of the whole system, thus enhancing the phosphorescence [47].

The temperature is the dominant factor affecting the pore size of the gelatin. Manipulating the aperture size of the gelatin allowed us to control the photo stimuli-responsive intensity of the CDs@Gelatin film. The BET results indicated that the internal pore sizes of the films dried at different temperatures are different (Fig. 3f). The aperture of the dried film decreased as the temperature increased from 110 °C to 170 °C. At 110 °C, oxygen gained the strongest permeability, and the photo stimuli-responsive phosphorescent phenomenon was the most evident. With the increase of radiation time, the photo stimuli-responsive intensity gradually increased and then stabilized. Unlike the reported photo-induced phosphorescence [15], our CDs@gelatin system does not require thermal treatment to inactivate phosphorescence. This is attributed to the special micro-nano porous structure in CDs@Gelatin film that facilitated oxygen diffusion smoothly, giving it high responsiveness.

As aforementioned, CDs@Gelatin film has the strongest phosphorescence intensity and the most visible photo stimuli-responsive phosphorescent phenomenon at 110 °C. Therefore, we determined the phosphorescence intensity of UV irradiation time at 110 °C at different times (Fig. S3a). It can be seen that with the increase of UV irradiation time, the phosphorescence intensity of CDs@Gelatin film first increased and then gradually stabilized, and reached the maximum value when the irradiation time was 24 s. In addition, we also found that the concentration of CDs also affected the phosphorescent intensity. The phosphorescent intensity of fully activated CDs@Gelatin film remained unchanged at low CDs concentration (Fig. S3b), but decreased significantly with the increase of CDs concentration. This is because excessive CDs concentration causes a self-quenching phenomenon, which affects the intensity of phosphorescent. Furthermore, the prepared CDs@Gelatin film exhibited the phosphorescence even when placed in a bare environment for more than 30 days (Fig. S4), which indicates its good stability.

Based on the aforementioned results, we have formulated a potential mechanism for the photo-induced phosphorescence of CDs@Gelatin film (Fig. 4 ). During the hydrothermal treatment process, the citric acid and urea are crosslinked, polymerized and carbonized, consequently produced the CDs that contain abundant functional groups such as CN and CO on their surface/edge. These functional groups are conducive to improving intersystem coupling efficiency [48]. After UV irradiation, the triplet oxygen infiltrating the composite film is gradually converted to singlet oxygen through a photo stimulus responsive process (II), leading to an anoxic region in the exposed region, thereby presenting a long phosphorescent afterglow at room temperature (III). After a certain period of local hypoxia, the oxygen diffused in the air will enter the composite film again (IV) (Fig. 4a). In addition, the temperature also affects the sensitivity of CDs@Gelatin film, annealing at 110 °C produces larger pore structure, which is conducive to oxygen flow. Crosslinking at either high temperature or low temperature blocks the binding water capability and makes oxygen difficult to penetrate, resulting in poor sensitivity of CDs@Gelatin film (Fig. 4b).

To further validate the role of special porous structure of gelatin, a different composite film was prepared similarly from CDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) as a control group. The phosphorescence intensity of the CDs@PVA film reached its maximum value at the beginning, but showed no change with the increase of illumination time (Fig. S3c), nor did it display photo stimuli-responsive phosphorescent phenomenon. This is different from the mesoporous structure of CDs@gelatin, where CDs@PVA films have very small pore sizes and almost no open-frame structure. When the film is illuminated by ultraviolet light, almost no oxygen flows in or out. Oxygen cannot quench phosphorescence [49]. Collectively, the special porous structure of gelatin gave rise to the photo stimuli-responsive phosphorescent phenomenon.

It is worth noting that UV light could activate photo stimuli-responsive phosphorescence, which allows us to print optical ink-free patterns using mask and lithography methods. By combining photoactivation and O2 inactivation of photo stimuli-responsive phosphorescent, reversible write-read-erase cycles with different long afterglow patterns could be easily achieved (Fig. 5 ). A green screen-printing diagram of oxygen-mediated photo stimulated responsive phosphorescence under photoexcitation (Fig. 5a). This makes the material naturally suitable for practical applications such as graphic encryption or anti-counterfeiting.

To illustrated its potential applications, we used a rabbit pattern to show how the green screen printing might be used. Briefly, the mixture solution of CDs and gelatin was added onto a clean glass substrate and then dried at 110 °C for 2 h to form the CDs@Gelatin films. The rabbit mask was then covered on the CDs@Gelatin film. The coated CDs@Gelatin film was irradiated with a 365 nm UV lamp for 2 min to complete the printing process. The printed information is invisible under daylight. However, after the UV light is turned off, the rabbit mold remains visible for a few seconds, indicating that the correct code can be accessed and used to encrypt and decrypt messages, but the rabbit pattern vanishes once the film is left in the air for 120 s. This is because under light irradiation, CDs embedded in the gelatin matrix continuously generate triplet excitons through in situ photodynamic processes, thereby removing triplet oxygen. After that, the irradiated area gradually became hypoxic, resulting in the photoactivation of phosphorescent. The photoinduced afterglow memory lasted in the pre-irradiated region for a long time before the membrane is re-permeated by ambient oxygen. When oxygen re-permeated the membrane, the afterglow memory disappeared again. We altered the rabbit pattern to the school emblem pattern for green lithography printing at this time, and the school emblem logo would show (Fig. 5b). It is interesting to note that the film can be reused for printing.

We developed a reversible photo stimuli-responsive composite film based on CDs and gelatin. The porous structure of gelatin plays an essential role in the photo-stimulating response of the composite film. The temperature greatly affected the pore size of gelatin, which further impacted the intensity of light stimulation response of the composite film. The photo response of the composite films was optimized at 110 °C, for the film had a pore size that allowed efficient oxygen exchange. Our research offers novel insight and promising perspective for fabricating stimuli responsive materials by integrating CDs within porous materials, which may allow us to find more applications for the next generation of metal-free optical materials in information storage and data security.

Qijun Li and Guoming Sun: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Project administration, Supervision, RuoChen Li and Qijun Li: Writing-Original Draft, Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Yi Wang: Validation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Hebei DHRSS Research Fund, China (No. E2019100005) and High-level Talents Research Start-up Project of Hebei University (521000981336, 521000981393).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Smart responsive phosphorescent materials for data recording and security protection
